did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a novel deep learning model named L2-EffCANet, which is based on the EfficientNetV2S architecture with Coordinate Attention (CA) and L2 regularization. This model was used for multi-class skin disease classification using the DermNet dataset, achieving a test accuracy of 71.78%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a novel deep learning model named L2-EffCANet for skin disease classification. It details the methodology (dataset preparation, augmentation, architecture design with Coordinate Attention and L2 regularization), experimental setup (hardware, software, training parameters), and results (test accuracy of 71.78% on the DermNet dataset). Although it includes a 'Related Works' section, its primary purpose is to report new technical contributions and validation, not to summarize prior literature.

title: The L2-EffCANet: A Novel Overfitting-Resistant EfficientNetV2S with Attention Mechanism and L2 Regularization for Skin Disease Classification
journal: International Journal of Online and Biomedical Engineering (iJOE)
brief_summary: This study developed L2-EffCANet, a novel deep learning model based on EfficientNetV2S with Coordinate Attention and L2 regularization, to address overfitting in multi-class skin disease classification. The model was trained and evaluated using the DermNet dataset, which contains 19,559 images across 23 distinct skin disease classes. Data augmentation and stratified splitting were employed to mitigate class imbalance and ensure robust evaluation. The proposed architecture achieved a test accuracy of 71.78%, along with a precision of 71.93% and an F1-score of 71.71%. Comparative analysis demonstrated that L2-EffCANet outperformed previous studies, including baseline EfficientNetV2S and other architectures like DenseNet201. The results indicate that integrating attention mechanisms and regularization effectively enhances the model's ability to classify complex and imbalanced dermatological data.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin disease diagnosis (23 distinct classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'multi-class skin disease classification' using the DermNet dataset with 23 classes. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Eczema, Psoriasis) rather than continuous values. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard for classification tasks. The loss function used is categorical cross-entropy, confirming a classification objective.

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising photographs of 23 distinct skin illnesses sourced from https://DermNetnz.org. The images are in JPEG format with RGB channels and vary in resolution, predominantly low resolution.

use.training: Used for training the L2-EffCANet model with on-the-fly data augmentation (rotation, shear, zoom, shift, flip) to mitigate class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (10% of total data) to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance, achieving a test accuracy of 71.78%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a held-out test set, and compared descriptively with previous studies in a table.

DermNet.notes: The L2-EffCANet model outperformed previous studies and baseline architectures. The training was terminated at epoch 47. The best validation accuracy of 71.17% was achieved at epoch 37. The model achieved a test accuracy of 71.78%, precision of 71.93%, and F1-score of 71.71%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using pre-trained EfficientNetV2S models

architecture_details.regularization: L2 regularization applied to kernel weights of the dense layer
layers: Dense layer with 256 units and ReLU activation

learning_rate.schedule: Reduced by 20% (multiplied by 0.8) if no validation accuracy improvement for 3 epochs; decreased to 10^-6 by epoch 20

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7-1165G7, 40 GB RAM, GeForce RTX 4060 Ti

preprocessing_techniques: Data splitting using stratified sampling to maintain uniform proportional distribution among classes

data_augmentation_techniques.details: All images were adjusted to a scale of 1/255.
Nearest neighbor filling mode used to preserve local structure without introducing artifacts.
Transformations were applied dynamically during the training process with a batch size of 16, rather than storing augmented results in memory.

conclusions: The proposed L2-EffCANet model, which integrates Coordinate Attention (CA) and L2 regularization into the EfficientNetV2S architecture, achieved a test accuracy of 71.78%, precision of 71.93%, and F1-score of 71.71% on the DermNet dataset.

L2-EffCANet demonstrated superior performance compared to previous studies, including a baseline DenseNet201 (68.97% accuracy), a previous EfficientNetV2S with L2 regularization on the kernel side (61.4% accuracy), and a genetic algorithm ensemble method (45.83% accuracy).
The integration of Coordinate Attention and L2 regularization effectively mitigated overfitting and addressed class imbalance issues inherent in multi-class skin disease classification.

Deep learning models utilizing transfer learning and regularization techniques can significantly aid in the early detection of skin diseases, thereby improving healthcare services, particularly in regions with limited dermatological resources.

The study acknowledges that while the model improves accuracy, challenges remain regarding the uneven representation of different skin conditions and the similar appearances of many skin disorders, which can negatively impact classification accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the DermNet dataset, which is sourced from a single website (DermNetnz.org), potentially introducing selection bias and limiting generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging devices.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with minority classes having very few samples (e.g., Urticaria with only 212 training images), which may negatively impact model sensitivity for rare conditions despite augmentation efforts.

The reported test accuracy of 71.78% is moderate for a 23-class multi-classification problem, indicating that the model still struggles with inter-class similarity and visual variations (lighting, resolution, skin color) common in dermatological images.

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a held-out subset of the training data source, raising concerns about over-optimistic performance estimates and lack of robustness testing on independent cohorts.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with previous studies, making it difficult to determine if the performance improvements are statistically significant.
The model was trained on low-resolution images predominantly, which may limit the extraction of fine-grained diagnostic features crucial for distinguishing between similar skin lesions.
There is a notable gap between training accuracy (~93.4%) and test accuracy (71.78%), indicating residual overfitting despite the use of L2 regularization and data augmentation.

The study acknowledges that challenges remain regarding the uneven representation of skin conditions and the similar appearances of many disorders, which are inherent limitations of the data rather than just the model architecture.
